name: Villin headpiece subdomain (HP36)
organism: Gallus gallus (chicken villin), used as a general model for the human/vertebrate villin headpiece
relevance: HP36 is the smallest known naturally occurring protein that folds cooperatively into a single, stable, well-defined three-helix bundle. Its small size, fast folding, and simple topology have made it the standard benchmark model system used to validate protein-folding simulation methods, including early distributed-computing folding projects. Villin itself is an actin-binding protein that structures the microvilli of intestinal and kidney epithelial cells, and villin expression is used diagnostically in pathology to help identify the tissue origin of certain tumors.
citation: McKnight CJ, Matsudaira PT, Kim PS. NMR structure of the 35-residue villin headpiece subdomain. Nat Struct Biol. 1997;4(3):180-4. PDB entry 1VII.
source: RCSB PDB entry 1VII (sequence and helix boundaries from the deposited NMR structure, as reported in the primary literature and multiple subsequent computational-biology studies)
note: Unlike amyloid-beta, HP36 has one well-defined native fold, which is why it was chosen: it allows a genuine 'distance to the folded target' comparison to be meaningful, whereas amyloid-beta does not have a single native answer to compare against. The 'reference' structure used for that comparison in this app is not the literal deposited NMR coordinates; it is a structure built by this app's own geometry engine using ideal alpha-helix torsion angles applied to the real helix boundaries listed above, plus a canonical (not individually rotamer-optimized) side-chain angle for every residue. It is a legitimate, real-geometry approximation of the correctly-folded shape, not a substitute for the literal experimental structure.
